coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as solid-state electron mediator and electron acceptor in S-scheme heterojunction for boosted photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zheng
Shi, Weilong
Shi, Yuxing
Sun, Haoran
Li, Lingling
Guo, Feng
Wen, Huabing
coredata.dc:description: Nowadays, it is still an attracting work to photocatalytic water splitting into hydrogen production using solar energy owing to its ecological and sustainable development significance. Herein, a ternary ZnIn2S4/carbon dots/g-C3N4 (ZIS/CDs/CN) S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst with solid-state electron mediator and electron acceptor dual roles of CDs (∼5 nm) as a co-catalyst are designed via a calcination method follow-up water bath process for boosted photocatalytic H2 evolution. Compared to individual ZIS and pure CN, the photocatalytic efficiency of H2 evolution over ZIS/CDs/CN is enhanced by about 2 and 10 times, respectively. Combined with density functional theory (DFT) calculations, the heterojunction type between ZIS and CN is determined to be S-scheme heterojunction. In order to corroborate the role of CDs in S-scheme heterojunction, ZnIn2S4/g-C3N4 (ZIS/CN) was synthesized for comparative experiments and characterization, and its promotion of S-scheme heterojunction was confirmed. Furthermore, ZIS/CDs/CN S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst still express excellent practicality in seawater environment and structural stability after four successive cycling reactions for photocatalytic H2 evolution. In addition, this bi-functional CDs is also applicable to S-scheme heterojunction constructed from other two different semiconductors, which enrich the construction method of later S-scheme heterojunctions and advance the development of S-scheme heterojunctions.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as solid-state electron mediator and electron acceptor in S-scheme heterojunction for boosted photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nowadays, it is still an attracting work to photocatalytic water splitting into hydrogen production using solar energy owing to its ecological and sustainable development significance. Herein, a ternary ZnIn2S4/carbon dots/g-C3N4 (ZIS/CDs/CN) S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst with solid-state electron mediator and electron acceptor dual roles of CDs (∼5 nm) as a co-catalyst are designed via a calcination method follow-up water bath process for boosted photocatalytic H2 evolution. Compared to individual ZIS and pure CN, the photocatalytic efficiency of H2 evolution over ZIS/CDs/CN is enhanced by about 2 and 10 times, respectively. Combined with density functional theory (DFT) calculations, the heterojunction type between ZIS and CN is determined to be S-scheme heterojunction. In order to corroborate the role of CDs in S-scheme heterojunction, ZnIn2S4/g-C3N4 (ZIS/CN) was synthesized for comparative experiments and characterization, and its promotion of S-scheme heterojunction was confirmed. Furthermore, ZIS/CDs/CN S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst still express excellent practicality in seawater environment and structural stability after four successive cycling reactions for photocatalytic H2 evolution. In addition, this bi-functional CDs is also applicable to S-scheme heterojunction constructed from other two different semiconductors, which enrich the construction method of later S-scheme heterojunctions and advance the development of S-scheme heterojunctions.

ce:section.ce:para: With the consumption of fossil fuels and the environmental pollution caused by their combustion products becoming increasingly serious problems, there is an urgent demand for developing the clean new energy source [1–6]. At present, compared with geographically restricted wind and water power, hydrogen production from water splitting appears to be faster and more convenient [7–11]. Generally, hydrogen production from water electrolysis is the main method, but since electricity itself is most derived from fossil fuels sources, it has the defects of high energy consumption, high cost, easy pollution and certain limitations [2]. In this regard, photocatalytic water splitting into hydrogen production is considered as the potential and ultimate solution with environmental protection, cleanness, economy and sustainability [12–16]. In addition, compared with scarce freshwater resources, seawater is particularly abundant [17,18], thus photocatalytic seawater splitting hydrogen production is largely to become the main mean from a long-term economic perspective in the future.

Currently, semiconductor-based photocatalysts mostly suffer from several drawbacks, such as low light absorption and utilization and fast recombination of electron-hole pairs [19–21]. In order to solve the above problems, much efforts have been devoted for enhancing photocatalytic performance, including crystal regulation [22,23], defect formation [24–26], elemental doping [27,28], loading precious metals [29,30], and heterojunction construction [31–35], etc. Among them, the construction of heterojunction is considered as an effective strategy to enhance light absorption and promote charge separation for improving photocatalytic H2 evolution performance [36–39]. Compared with conventional type-II heterojunctions, S-scheme heterojunctions retain and enhance the redox capability of both semiconductors, while the built-in electric field and the bending of the energy band also promote the charge transfer at the heterojunctions interface, all of which contribute to the photocatalytic efficiency [40]. S-scheme heterojunctions usually contain a reduction photocatalyst (RP) and an oxidation photocatalyst (OP) semiconductor. Comparatively, RP has a higher Fermi energy level, and upon contact, electrons are spontaneously transferred from RP side to OP side, then RP side is positively charged due to the loss of electrons, and OP side is negatively charged by the gain of electrons [40,41]. This charge difference leads to the formation of a built-in electric field and the bending of the energy band, which further accelerates the charge separation. After photoexcitation, electron-hole separation occurs at both RP and OP sides, and combined effect of the built-in electric field and energy band bending results to recombination of electrons on the conduction band (CB) of OP side and holes on the valence band potential (VB) of RP side, while retaining powerful electrons and holes on CB of the RP side and on VB of OP side [40–42], which makes this S-scheme heterojunction to enhance the redox capability of the semiconductors. For instance, Xiao et al. [43] designed CeO2/BiOI S-scheme heterojunction, for removing Hg0 under visible light, and Hg0 removal efficiency of best sample is about 1.5 and 2 times higher than pure CeO2 and BiOI, respectively; Meng et al. [44] constructed TiO2/polydopamine S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst with enhanced CO2 reduction, which is ascribed to enhancement of optical absorption, improved CO2 adsorption capacity and efficient separation and transfer of photo-induced carriers by the S-scheme heterojunction; Chen et al. [31] synthesized BiVO4/g-C3N4 S-scheme heterojunction for efficient removal of glyphosate, and the formed interface S-scheme heterojunction could maintain strong redox potentials, which significantly elevate its photocatalytic activity.

Carbon dots (CDs) as a new carbon nanomaterial have received extensive attention in the field of photocatalysis due to the fascinating natures, such as brilliant visible responses, and excellent electron transfer and storage functions [45–51]. Based on this, CDs is regularly compounded with other semiconductors to compensate for the inherent defects of photocatalysts and enhance their photocatalytic activities [47,48,52]. This outstanding electron transfer and storage property fit well with the charge transfer in internal electric field (IEF) and photocatalytic reaction in energy band of S-scheme heterojunctions. However, currently, this combined program of CDs and S-scheme heterojunction has not been reported, and role of CDs in S-scheme heterojunction has also not been specific discussion and analysis.

In this work, we first designed a ternary ZnIn2S4/carbon dots/g-C3N4 (ZIS/CDs/CN) S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst with CDs as a co-catalyst and the type of heterojunction between ZIS and CN, which is proved to be S-scheme by DFT and some related characterization. To illustrate the role of CDs in S-scheme heterojunctions, analogous ZIS/CN were synthesized, and relevant photocatalytic hydrogen evolution behaviors and characterizations were also compared under the same conditions, the results indicated that CDs played a positive role in in S-scheme heterojunctions. Combined with S-scheme heterojunction formation steps, the electron transfers and storage features of CDs could promote the formation of IEF and the reduction reaction in energy band, respectively. The corresponding role of CDs in the S-scheme heterojunction and photocatalytic mechanism of ZIS/CDs/CN S-scheme heterojunction were investigated in detail.

All chemical reagents are of analytical grade without further purification. Urea (99%, AR), indium chloride tetrahydrate (InCl3·4H2O, 99%, AR), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, 98%, AR), thioacetamide (TAA, 99%, AR) and glycerin (99%, AR) are purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd.

CDs were prepared by a typical method of electrochemical etching graphite rods that reported according to a previous report [53]. The preparation of CDs is mainly based on the etching of electrode graphite rods, which are continuously separated into graphite carbon fragments, and then select small carbon points by centrifugation. Specifically, two graphite rods inserted into ultrapure water were applied a bias of 30 V using a direct current (DC) power supply. After 20 days of reaction, collected and washed the black CDs solution in the reactor. Finally, the CDs solution was freeze-dried to obtain the target CDs powder.

Different masses of CDs (3.03, 6.06 and 18.55 mg) were evenly mixed with 10 g urea. Next, the mixture was directly heated to 550 °C for 2 h at a heating rate of 5 °C/min in air atmosphere. After grinding, the CDs/CN composite product powders with different mass ratios of CDs were prepared and marked as CDs/CN (x wt.%), which the x values were 0.5, 1 and 3, respectively. The g-C3N4 (CN) powder was synthesized via same method without adding the CDs. Unless otherwise marked, CDs/CN is indicated as CDs/CN (1 wt%) below.

The ZIS/CDs/CN composites were synthesized via a low temperature solvothermal method (see Scheme 1 ). Firstly, appropriate amount of CDs/CN (9.2, 19.4, 30.9 and 43.7 mg), ZnCl2 (544 mg), InCl3⋅4H2O (1172 mg) and TAA (600 mg) were dispersed into a mixed water/glycerol (160 mL/40 mL) solution. After stirring continuously for half an hour, the resulting mixture solution was heated at 80 °C for 2 h. Finally, the products were collected and washed using ethanol, and dried at 60 °C overnight. The obtained composites were denoted as ZIS/CDs/CN (y wt.%), where the y values were 5, 10, 15 and 20 represented the mass percentages of CDs/CN used in the ZIS/CDs/CN composites. Unless otherwise marked, ZIS/CDs/CN is indicated as ZIS/CDs/CN (10 wt%) below. Pure ZIS was synthesized by the same method without adding CDs/CN, and ZIS/CN was composited by the same means of replacing CDs/CN with CN, and ZIS/CDs was also prepared with addition CDs into the preparation of ZIS.
Relevant details of the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution experiments and characterization refer to Supporting Information .

Fig. 1 a exhibits the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of as-synthesized photocatalysts. Pure CN and CDs/CN show two characteristic diffraction peaks, corresponding to the (002) and (100) lattice planes of g-C3N4, which are attributed to aromatic ring structure units and reduplicative tri-s-triazine units, respectively [47,54,55]. Notably, compared with CN, the higher diffraction peak (002) of CDs/CN shifts from 28.1° to 27.6° and another peak shifts from 13.8° to 13.1°. Moreover, the (002) characteristic peak intensity of CDs/CN is reduced at a certain extent after the addition of CDs. The above results indicate that the presence of CDs hinders the polymerization of urea and reduces the crystallinity of CN, and also suggest that CDs are successfully incorporated into the CN matrix [54]. To ZIS, the characteristic diffraction peaks at 20.6°, 27.5°, 31.9°, 41.0°, 47.5°, 51.7° and 56.1° are pertained to (006), (102), (104), (108), (110), (116) and (202) lattice planes of ZnIn2S4 (JCPDS No. 72-0773) [56]. Furthermore, the characteristic diffraction peaks of ZIS/CDs/CN composite sample have no much difference with pure ZIS, which due to the low incorporation of CDs/CN. Significantly, the diffraction peak of ZIS/CDs/CN at around 28° is substantially attributed to (102) plane of ZIS and (002) plane of CN (inset of Fig. 1a). In order to further analyze the chemical structure of as-prepared samples, Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was carried out and the corresponding spectra are displayed in Fig. 1b. The bare CN possesses characteristic bands in 3050–3300 cm−1 region are attributed to N-H tensile vibration and carboxylic acid O–H stretching, respectively [57]. Moreover, the characteristic absorption peaks in the interval of 1204–1637 cm−1 are corresponded to the stretching of sp3 C–N and sp2 C=N in CN heterocycles of g-C3N4 [58–60], and the peak at 814 cm−1 is caused by triazine-triazine structure of g-C3N4 [61–63]. For pure ZIS, two weak peaks at 1624 and 1400 cm−1 are assigned to surface bound water molecules and hydroxyl groups, respectively [29]. However, compare with CN and CDs/CN samples, the peak positions of ZIS/CDs/CN composite sample have no obvious change, expect for reduced absorption peaks intensities, owing to relatively low percentage of CDs/CN in ZIS/CDs/CN ternary composite. No relevant absorption peaks are observed in other composite samples including CDs, ascribing to low content of CDs in composite. The above results can also indicate the successful synthesis of the ZIS/CDs/CN complex.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) is used to explore the microstructure and morphology characteristics of prepared samples. As exhibited in Fig. 2 a, ZIS shows a structure with thin and staggered nanosheets. In addition, pure CN (Fig. 2b) exhibits a clear fold nanosheet structure, and structure of CDs/CN (Fig. 2c and Fig.S1) is similar to the CN, indicating that the general structure of CN is unchanged after addition of CDs. Compared to ZIS, ZIS/CDs/CN (Fig. 2d) displays a stacked sheet- on-sheet structure of ZIS covering CDs/CN. Obviously, the 2D/2D structure provides a great deal of heterojunction interfaces, which is beneficial to improve photocatalytic activity. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the ZIS/CDs/CN composite also demonstrate that CDs/CN (light-colored areas) grows on ZIS nanosheet structure (dark-colored areas) (Fig. 2e). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image further proves more details about the heterojunction interfaces of ZIS/CDs/CN (Fig. 2f). Specifically, the obvious heterojunction interface (red dotted line) can be seen between ZIS nanosheet and disordered laminar-like CN containing substantial CDs with the size of around 5 nm. In addition, the clear lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.32 nm and 0.21 nm are corresponded to (102) crystal planes of hexagonal ZnIn2S4 and (002) crystal plane of CDs [29,56,64]. The HAADF-STEM (Fig. 2g) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX in Fig. 2h) spectra reveal that the ZIS/CDs/CN composite contains C, N, Zn, In and S elements, which further confirms CDs/CN grows on the ZIS nanosheet structure.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is utilized to analyze the chemical status and surface chemical component of elements in synthesized ZIS/CDs/CN composite. The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 3 a) demonstrates that ZIS/CDs/CN composite sample includes Zn, In, C, N and S elements. In Fig. 3b, three main peaks at 284.7, 286.4 and 288.7 eV of the C 1s spectrum are corresponded to the sp2 C-C bonds, C=N bonds and the sp2 hybridized carbon in the N-containing aromatic rings (N-C=N) of CN [29,64]. Notably, two other weak peaks at 285.6 and 288.1 eV can be assigned to the C-O and C=O of CDs, which also proves the existence of CDs at some extent [65]. The high resolution XPS N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) is divided into four peaks at 398.8, 399.5, 401.1 and 404.9 eV, respectively. The former three major peaks can be attributed to the sp2-hybridized N in the triazine units (C-N=C), tertiary N bonded to carbon atoms (N-(C)3), and amino functional groups bonded with an H atom (C-N-H), respectively; and another small peak belongs to π-excitations in the heterocycles [29,47]. The Zn 2p spectrum (Fig. 3d) shows two binding energies at 1021.7 and 1044.8 eV, which can be assigned to Zn 2p3/2 and In 2p5/2, respectively [29,56]. The In 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 4 e) presents two peaks at 445.1 and 452.6 eV, which can be attributed to In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2, respectively [29,56]. Meanwhile, combined with the XPS spectral peaks of Zn and In, it can be inferred that their oxidation states are +2 and +3, respectively, which are consistent with those in ZIS [29,66]. For the S 2p spectrum in Fig. 3f, the two peaks at 163.0 (2p3/2) and 161.8 eV (2p5/2) confirm that the chemical state of S in the composite is −2, which also matches the valence states of Zn and In [67,68]. To sum up, the XPS results indicate the successful synthesis of the ZIS/CDs/CN composite.

Moreover, the optical properties of ZIS, CN, CDs/CN, ZIS/CN, and ZIS/CDs/CN are researched by UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 4), and the colors of as-prepared samples are shown in Fig.S2. Compared with the pure CN, the absorption edge of CDs/CN is steeper, which demonstrates that absorption capacity of CDs/CN in the visible range is enhanced. As the CDs is introduced, the color of CDs/CN also becomes darker, which coincides with the above analysis of UV–vis absorption spectra. However, the maximum absorption edge of the CDs/CN sample has no apparent shift (dotted line), indicating that the reason for the improved photocatalytic performance of CDs/CN is that CDs acts as a co-catalyst [69]. Compared with ZIS, CN, and ZIS/CN, the optical absorption edges of ZIS/CDs/CN composite display red shifts, and the color of ZIS/CDs/CN is darker, which implies that more visible light can be harvested in ZIS/CDs/CN composite. The band gap of semiconductor photocatalysts is obtained from the Eqs. (1) [70]:
(1)
α h υ = A
( h υ - E g )
2
where α, h, υ, Eg, and A represent the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, band gap energy, and a constant. The analysis results are shown in Fig.S3, band gaps of CN and CDs/CN both are 2.84 eV and that of ZIS is 2.62 eV, respectively.

The photocatalytic H2 evolution performance of as-prepared photocatalysts is evaluated under visible-light irradiation. As shown in Fig.S4a, a certain number of CDs in CDs/CN composite significantly can improve the photocatalytic activity of CN, and the optimal hydrogen production performance of CDs/CN (1.0 wt%) sample (∼21.33 mmol/g) is about six times than that of pure CN (∼3.58 mmol/g). However, the hydrogen production efficiency of CDs/CN (3.0 wt%) (∼11.54 mmol/g) is even lower than that of CDs/CN (0.5 wt%) (∼14.65 mmol/g) with the continuous increase of CDs, which can be ascribed to excessive CDs in composite could affect the absorption of visible light for CN [47]. Based on the above results, the best sample CDs/CN (1.0 wt%) is selected and adopted different mass ratios to composite with ZIS to construct the ternary heterojunction, and the H2 evolution performance was further tested. Fig.S4b exhibits that the activity of the ZIS/CDs/CN heterojunction photocatalysts first increases then decreases after the ratio of CDs/CN exceeds 10 wt% with the addition of CDs/CN, which likely ascribe to excess CDs/CN forms a light barrier that hinders light absorption of ZIS/CDs/CN heterojunction. In addition, Fig. 5 a presents the time courses of photocatalytic H2 evolution of as-prepared photocatalysts, and the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of them is exhibited in Fig. 5c. As can be seen, pure CN only a trace amount of hydrogen is produced and the H2 evolution rate is ∼1.78 mmol/h/g, which can be attributed to easy recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs of CN. After compounding with CDs and ZIS, the H2 evolution performance of ZIS/CDs/CN heterojunction photocatalysts have been greatly improved combining the experimental results. Among them, ZIS/CDs/CN display the highest H2 evolution rate (∼17.58 mmol/h/g), which is about 10 times higher than the pure CN and 2 times higher than the pure ZIS (Fig. 5c). In order to prove the role of CDs in the ZIS/CDs/CN heterojunction, ZIS/CN is synthesized as a control sample. XRD, FT-IR analysis and SEM image of ZIS/CN sample are revealed in Fig.S5-6, the consequent certify that the structure of ZIS/CDs/CN is not much different from ZIS/CN without adding CDs barely. Next, a comparative photocatalytic H2 evolution performance of ZIS/CN and ZIS/CDs/CN are tested under the same conditions and ratios and the findings are displayed in Fig. 5b and the photocatalytic H2 evolution rates of ZIS/CN and ZIS/CDs (supplemental control groups) are exhibited in Fig. 5c. Without the reinforcement of heterojunctions, ZIS/CDs composite exhibits better photocatalytic activity than phase ZIS. Notably, after comparison, photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of ZIS/CDs/CN is about 3 mmol/g/h higher than that of ZIS/CN, which indicates that transport of photo-generated carriers is elevated due to the introduction of CDs in heterojunctions.

Meanwhile, in order to broaden the application range of as-prepared photocatalyst and improve its practicality, the same photocatalytic H2 evolution experiment is carried out in seawater (Fig. 5d). The results show that the as-obtained ZIS/CDs/CN ternary composite photocatalyst still manifest satisfactory photocatalytic activity in the seawater environment.

Based on the above experimental results, the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of H2 evolution on ZIS/CDs/CN ternary composite photocatalyst under the light irradiation at wavelength of 420 nm is summarized on Fig. 6 a. The AQEs are estimated to be 12.73 % and 9.21 % in the water and seawater environment, respectively, which demonstrates that the ZIS/CDs/CN heterojunction displays an excellent photo-produced carrier utilization efficiency. Moreover, the photocatalytic H2 evolution stability of ZIS/CDs/CN was evaluated by carrying out under water and seawater environments, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 6b, the as-prepared ZIS/CDs/CN heterojunction still maintains a high photocatalytic activity within four cycles experiments. Then, by the comparison of XRD, FT-IR analysis and SEM images over the samples before and after photocatalysis (Fig. 6c-d, Fig.S7), the structure and functional group of the composite photocatalyst has not changed much, indicating the excellent stability of ZIS/CDs/CN sample.

Transient photocurrent measurements and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used to study the excitation and transfer of photo-generated charge carriers. As exhibited in Fig. 7 a, all as-prepared samples disclose relatively stable photocurrent response for several on–off cycles under visible light irradiation. These curves all display photocurrent peak and decrease to zero immediately after the irradiation started and end, which is caused by the separation of electron–hole pairs at the interface of electrode/electrolyte [17]. By contrast, the ZIS/CDs/CN heterojunction demonstrate higher photocurrents than ZIS, CN, CDs/CN and ZIS/CN, implying a more efficient transfer and separation of photo-generated electron–hole pairs due to the advantages of heterojunctions. For ZIS/CN and ZIS/CDs/CN, the highest transient photocurrent density of ZIS/CDs/CN further confirms the promoting role of CDs in heterojunction. Similarly, EIS results of the as-prepared samples (Fig. 5b) also indicate that the interfacial electron-transfer resistance in ZIS/CDs/CN is much smaller than that pure ZIS, CN and CDs/CN. Comparing with ZIS/CN, a similar conclusion with transient photocurrent measurements can also be drawn by the interfacial electron-transfer resistance of ZIS/CN is higher than ZIS/CDs/CN, demonstrating that addition of CDs in the composite could enhance transfer of photo-generated charge carriers. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra reveal the separation and recombination tendency of the photo-generated electron–hole pairs [60]. Fig. 7c indicates that the PL peak density of ZIS/CDs/CN and ZIS/CN are much lower than that of CN, showing a lower recombination rate of photo-generated electron–hole pairs due to formation of the heterojunction. Moreover, the peak intensity of ZIS/CDs/CN is lower than ZIS/CN, which is attributed to the positive effect of CDs to the heterojunction. Fig. 7d shows time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay spectra of CN, ZIS/CN and ZIS/CDs/CN. Fitting by multi-exponential kinetics function (Table S1), the fluorescence lifetime values of CN, ZIS/CN and ZIS/CDs/CN are 7.15, 3.61 and 3.01 ns, respectively. The shorter fluorescence lifetimes of ZIS/CDs/CN and ZIS/CN demonstrate faster photo-excited transfer, which benefit from the blessing of the heterojunction between ZIS and CN; simultaneously, the fluorescence lifetimes of ZIS/CDs/CN is shorter than ZIS/CN, which illustrates the promotion of the heterojunction after the introduction of CDs.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations are adopted to directly evaluate work functions and charge densities, which can be inferred to the charge transfer mode between the interfaces of two semiconductors [71,72]. The calculated results of ZIS (102) surface, CN (001) surface are shown in Fig. 8 . The calculated work functions of the ZIS (102) facet and monolayer CN (001) are 6.16 and 4.29 eV, respectively. Meanwhile, the Fermi level (Ef) of CN is higher than that of ZIS, which indicates that in the case of this Fermi level difference, electrons could spontaneously transfer from CN to ZIS upon contact between two semiconductors until this energy level are balanced [43,44,73]. Consequently, an internal electric field is established from CN to ZIS, which facilitates the directional transfer of electrons and effectively prevents the recombination of electrons and holes during the photocatalytic reaction processes. Combined with the reports of previous studies [31,43,44,73], the type of heterojunction between ZIS and CN can be classified as S-scheme heterojunction.

Electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis is conducted to monitor superoxide radical (•O2 −) and hydroxyl radical (•OH) generation before and after photocatalytic reactions. For ZIS/CN and ZIS/CDs/CN composite (Fig. 9 a-b), no ESR signals are observed in the dark, while strong ESR signals from DMPO-•O2 − and DMPO-•OH could be detected after light irradiation, suggesting that ·O2 − and •OH radicals can be generated on ZIS/CN and ZIS/CDs/CN composite. Based on the same active radicals (Fig. 9b), the ESR signal intensity of ZIS/CDs/CN significantly higher than that of ZIS/CN, which illustrates that ZIS/CDs/CN heterojunction could produce more active radicals on the same photocatalytic reaction conditions, and laterally argues that CDs could further accelerate the electron transport and transfer in the heterojunction during photocatalytic reactions.

Additionally, Mott–Schottky (M-S) measurements could evaluate the CB positions of ZIS and CN (Fig. 10 a-b). It can be seen from the figure that both ZIS and CN are n-type semiconductors because the slopes of their M-S curves are positive [74]. Furthermore, the derived flat-band potentials (Efb) of ZIS and CN are estimated to be −0.80 and −1.21 V, respectively. In general, in n-type semiconductors, the CB is −0.20 V lower than Efb [29], so the CB of ZIS and CN were determined to be −1.32 eV and −1.00 eV. Based on the above analysis, the VB potential positions of CN and ZIS can be calculated using the Eqs. (2) [29]:
(2)
E
CB
=
E
VB
-
E g
where EVB is the VB potential, ECB is the CB potential and Eg is the band gap energy. Therefore, the VB of ZIS and CN are 1.52 and 1.62 eV, respectively, and the final energy diagrams of CN and ZIS are shown in Fig.S8. Combined with the above results, the possible charge transfer across hetero-interface over ZIS/CDs/CN S-scheme heterojunction under irradiation is illustrated in Fig. 10c. DFT calculations reveal that the Fermi level of CN is higher than that of ZIS before contact (Fig. 10c1). After contact, due to the difference in Fermi energy levels, the electrons tend to migrate spontaneously from CN to ZIS until their Fermi levels reach equilibrium. Furthermore, the directional movement of electrons bends the energy band and generates an internal electric field (IEF) between the interfaces of two semiconductors (Fig. 10c2) [41,71]. In this case, CDs in CN acts a role of solid-state electron mediator, to accelerate charge transfer in the ternary S-scheme ZIS/CDs/CN heterojunction [75,76]. In Fig. 10c3, under irradiation, electrons are photo-excited from the VB to the CB of ZIS and CN, respectively. By the combined effect of IEF and band bending, the photo-generated electrons in CB of ZIS prefer to recombine with the holes in VB of CN, which promotes the transfer of photo-generated electrons from VB to CB of ZIS and CN. This process enhances the charge separation efficiency and redox ability of the photocatalysis [71–73]. Noteworthy, CDs in CN not only acts a role of solid-state electron mediator at the heterojunction interface of ZIS and CN, but also is an electron acceptor combining H+ forms H2 molecules under light conditions [47,54].

In summary, a ternary ZIS/CDs/CN S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst is prepared and applied to photocatalytic H2 evolution. The photocatalytic H2 evolution performance of as-prepared ZIS/CDs/CN photocatalyst is approximately 2 and 10 times higher than ZIS and CN, respectively. Experimental characterization and related theoretical results indicate that formation of S-scheme heterojunction and solid-state electron mediator and electron acceptor dual roles of CDs on S-scheme heterojunction together promote the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalyst. The bi-functional CDs further enhances the photocatalytic activity based on S-scheme heterojunction, which not only acts as an “electronic bridge” between the two semiconductors to accelerate the formation of IEF and the bending of the energy band, but also acts as an electron acceptor in the CB to promote the photo-reduction reaction. Apparently, the bi-functional CDs as a potential co-catalyst can be extended to the other two semiconductor-built S-scheme heterojunction in the field of photocatalysis.
